content: AUD/USD denotes the exchange rate quoted as the amount of USD per 1 AUD.
GBP/USD denotes the exchange rate quoted as the amount of USD per 1 GBP.
CAD/USD denotes the exchange rate quoted as the amount of USD per 1 CAD.
CHF/USD denotes the exchange rate quoted as the amount of USD per 1 CHF.
CNY/USD denotes the exchange rate quoted as the amount of USD per 1 CNY.
JPY/USD denotes the exchange rate quoted as the amount of USD per 1 JPY.
SGD/USD denotes the exchange rate quoted as the amount of USD per 1 SGD.
NZD/USD denotes the exchange rate quoted as the amount of USD per 1 NZD.
The exchange rate tends to be positively correlated with the interest rate differential between the domestic and foreign currencies.
Higher interest rate differential leads to a higher exchange rate (domestic currency appreciation).
DXY refers to the U.S. Dollar Index, an index that measures the value of the U.S. dollar against a basket of major currencies.
The U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) is negatively correlated with most XXX/USD exchange rates.
A higher DXY generally corresponds to a lower XXX/USD exchange rate, indicating foreign currency depreciation against the U.S. dollar.
VIX refers to the CBOE Volatility Index, a measure of expected stock market volatility.
Higher VIX values indicate higher market risk.
AUD/USD, GBP/USD, CAD/USD, CNY/USD, SGD/USD, and NZD/USD typically decrease when VIX rises.
CHF/USD generally shows little change or may slightly increase when VIX rises, as the Swiss franc is considered a safe-haven currency.
JPY/USD generally shows limited change or may slightly increase when VIX rises, as the Japanese yen is also a safe-haven currency.
U.S. NFCI refers to the National Financial Conditions Index, which measures U.S. financial market conditions.
Higher U.S. NFCI values indicate tighter U.S. financial conditions and lower risk appetite.
AUD/USD, NZD/USD, CAD/USD, GBP/USD, CNY/USD, and SGD/USD typically decrease when U.S. NFCI rises.
CHF/USD, as a safe-haven currency, generally shows limited change when U.S. NFCI rises.
JPY/USD, as a safe-haven currency, generally shows limited change when U.S. NFCI rises.
